content.assessment: T6 formalizes discretionary administrative gatekeeping rather than undertaking the kind of full structural reform Poland pursued. The Polish lens's distinction between structural mechanism and administrative/procedural levers is again relevant: T6, like T1 and T2, works within the existing tracking architecture rather than changing the age or existence of tracking itself, meaning it cannot be expected to replicate Poland's documented gap-reduction effects from structural delay, though it also avoids Poland's reversal risks.
opportunities: Because it is administrative rather than legislative, T6 could be implemented and adjusted incrementally without the all-or-nothing transition risk that characterized Poland's abrupt 2017 reversal (F-live-polish_reform-04)
strengths: As an administrative rather than statutory mechanism, T6 carries lower legal-reversal risk than Poland's legislated structural changes, which were fully reversed via ordinary legislative process in 2017 (F-live-polish_reform-04)
threats: Purely preventive/administrative tools, lacking the structural force of Poland's tracking-age change, may be insufficient to produce comparable distributional effects, and could be reversed or under-enforced with the same ease as any discretionary administrative practice, unlike a statutorily embedded structural reform
weaknesses: T6 addresses only marginal future segregation risk (new/expanded tracks) and does not touch the existing tracking-age structure, which Polish evidence suggests is the primary lever associated with documented socio-economic gap reduction (F-live-polish_reform-03)
